lessons.title: Compliance Architecture: TCPA, CTIA, and Carrier Rules
lessons.title_es: Arquitectura de cumplimiento: TCPA, CTIA y reglas de operadores
lessons.description: Build a legally defensible SMS program that survives audits and carrier filtering.
lessons.description_es: Construye un programa de SMS legalmente sólido que supere auditorías y filtros de operadores.
lessons.content:
The Compliance Stack

SMS marketing sits at the intersection of three overlapping rule sets: TCPA (federal statute), CTIA guidelines (industry self-regulation), and individual carrier policies (AT&T, T-Mobile, Verizon). Violating any layer can result in fines ($500–$1,500 per message under TCPA), short-code suspension, or 10DLC campaign rejection.

Opt-In Requirements

Every compliant SMS program starts with express written consent. The consent must be:

  • Unconditioned — you cannot require SMS opt-in to receive a product or service
  • Program-specific — a single opt-in cannot cover multiple message programs
  • Documented — store timestamp, IP, opt-in source URL, and message copy shown at opt-in

The Double Opt-In (DOI) flow is the safest architecture: user submits phone → system sends a confirmation SMS → user replies YES → contact is marked sms_confirmed: true. DOI reduces spam complaints by 60–80% and provides an audit trail.

10DLC Registration

All business SMS traffic in the US must flow through 10-digit long codes (10DLC) registered with The Campaign Registry (TCR). Registration requires:

  • Brand registration (EIN, legal name, vertical)
  • Campaign use case (marketing, transactional, 2FA, etc.)
  • Sample message copy — must match actual messages sent

Throughput on registered 10DLC campaigns: 3–10 MPS (messages per second) depending on Trust Score. Unregistered traffic is filtered at >99% by major carriers.

Keyword Infrastructure

Every SMS program must support these mandatory keywords:

  • STOP / UNSUBSCRIBE — immediate opt-out, no further messages
  • HELP — returns program name, support contact
  • START / YES — re-opt-in after previous stop

Build your keyword handler as a prefix router: strip the inbound message, uppercase it, match against a keyword table before any other processing. Log every opt-out immediately and propagate to all sending lists within 10 seconds — TCPA requires honoring opt-outs within 10 business days, but best practice is real-time.

Message Hygiene Rules

To maintain deliverability: keep messages under 160 characters to avoid concatenation fees; include your brand name in every outbound message; never include URL shorteners on untrusted domains (carrier filters flag these); maintain a complaint rate below 0.3%.

lessons.title: Keyword Triggers and Conversational Flows
lessons.title_es: Disparadores por palabra clave y flujos conversacionales
lessons.description: Design trigger-based SMS flows that feel conversational and drive action.
lessons.description_es: Diseña flujos de SMS basados en disparadores que se sientan conversacionales y generen acción.
lessons.content:
Trigger Architecture

A keyword trigger is any inbound message that matches a predefined pattern and fires an automated response sequence. The trigger engine has three layers: exact match, intent match (regex or NLP), and fallback handler.

Design your trigger map before writing any copy. A complete trigger map includes: trigger keyword, match type (exact/fuzzy), response message, next state, and timeout behavior.

The State Machine Model

Every SMS conversation is a finite state machine. Each contact has a current state stored server-side (e.g., awaiting_preference, in_drip_sequence, opted_out). Inbound messages are evaluated against the current state first, then against global keywords.

State transition example:

  • Contact receives promo → replies INFO → state moves to info_requested → system sends product details + follow-up question
  • Contact replies PRICE → system sends pricing card + CTA → state moves to pricing_sent
  • No reply in 24h → state moves to inactive → drip sequence pauses

Intent-Based Routing

Beyond exact keywords, implement intent classification using one of two approaches:

  • Regex patterns: Fast, predictable, zero API cost. Example: / (how much|price|cost|rate) /i routes to pricing flow.
  • LLM classification: Send inbound text to a small model (Claude Haiku) with a classification prompt. Returns intent label + confidence. Use when conversations are freeform or multi-intent.

For most SMS programs, regex covers 80% of cases. Reserve LLM routing for high-value flows (appointment scheduling, quote requests) where accuracy justifies latency.

Flow Construction Best Practices

Keep each SMS turn to a single ask. Never ask two questions in one message. Use numbered reply options for branching decisions: "Reply 1 for Tuesday, 2 for Wednesday, 3 to skip." This dramatically improves reply rates vs open-ended prompts.

Build a graceful exit at every node. If the contact's reply doesn't match any expected pattern, send a friendly clarification message and offer to connect them with a human. Dead ends kill engagement and increase opt-out rates.

Track flow completion rate per sequence. Any flow with completion below 40% needs a rewrite — usually the issue is message length, unclear CTA, or asking for too much commitment too early.

lessons.title: Drip Sequence Design Across SMS, Instagram DM, and Facebook Messenger
lessons.title_es: Diseño de secuencias drip en SMS, Instagram DM y Facebook Messenger
lessons.description: Build unified drip campaigns that coordinate across three messaging channels without feeling spammy.
lessons.description_es: Construye campañas drip unificadas que coordinen en tres canales de mensajería sin parecer spam.
lessons.content:
Cross-Channel Drip Architecture

A multi-platform drip sequence treats each messaging channel as a lane in a unified contact journey. The orchestration layer (your CRM or sequence engine) owns the timeline; individual channel adapters handle delivery mechanics and rate limits.

Core components: Contact Profile (unified record with channel opt-in status per platform), Sequence Definition (JSON or YAML defining steps, delays, channel preference), Delivery Router (picks optimal channel per step based on engagement history), Event Bus (propagates opens/replies/opt-outs across channels).

Channel Characteristics

  • SMS: Highest open rate (98% within 3 min). Best for time-sensitive CTAs, reminders, short confirmations. Character limit enforces brevity. Opt-out is legally binding across all SMS sends.
  • Instagram DM: Requires prior interaction or paid messaging entry point. 24-hour messaging window after last user interaction (Meta rules). Best for visual content delivery, product showcases, community-driven brands.
  • Facebook Messenger: Similar 24-hour window rule. Broader demographic (35–65+). Supports rich cards, carousels, quick reply buttons. Better for complex decision flows.

Sequence Blueprint: 7-Day Lead Nurture

Day 0 (immediate): SMS — confirm opt-in, deliver lead magnet link.
Day 1: IG DM — welcome message with visual brand intro, soft value prop.
Day 3: SMS — one specific pain-point message with a question ("Are you currently doing X?").
Day 5: FB Messenger — case study card with quick-reply: "Want to see how? Yes / Not now".
Day 7: SMS — direct offer with deadline. Hard CTA.

Suppression and Fatigue Logic

A contact who replies on any channel should pause other-channel sends for 24 hours. This prevents the "I just replied on Instagram and then got a text 10 minutes later" experience that feels robotic and drives opt-outs.

Implement engagement scoring: contacts who open but never reply score lower than contacts who reply. After 14 days of zero engagement across all channels, move contacts to a re-engagement sequence (one message every 7 days, max 3 attempts) before suppressing permanently.

Platform-specific unsubscribes must propagate immediately. An Instagram DM opt-out should flag the contact as ig_dm_blocked and remove them from all IG steps in active sequences within 5 minutes.

lessons.title: Analytics, A/B Testing, and Deliverability Optimization
lessons.title_es: Análisis, pruebas A/B y optimización de entregabilidad
lessons.description: Measure what matters in multi-channel messaging and systematically improve performance.
lessons.description_es: Mide lo que importa en mensajería multicanal y mejora el rendimiento sistemáticamente.
lessons.content:
The Four Messaging KPIs That Drive Decisions

Most teams track open rates and stop there. The four metrics that actually predict program health:

  • Opt-Out Rate per Campaign: Industry benchmark <0.5%. Spikes indicate message-list mismatch or send-time problems. Track per campaign, not just program-wide.
  • Reply Rate: The signal that your message was compelling enough to act on. Benchmark: 5–10% for promotional SMS, 15–25% for conversational flows.
  • Sequence Completion Rate: What % of enrollees finish the full drip. Low completion = drop-off at a specific step. Identify the leak step.
  • Attributed Revenue per Message: Connect UTM-tagged links and CRM deal stages. Divide revenue credited to the sequence by total messages sent.

A/B Testing Methodology

SMS A/B testing is tricky because small changes have large effects. Run tests with at minimum 500 contacts per variant and statistical significance at 95% before calling a winner.

Test one variable at a time. Priority order: 1) CTA phrasing (reply Y vs. reply YES vs. tap link), 2) Send time (10am vs 6pm local), 3) Message length (short punchy vs. context-rich), 4) Offer framing (save $X vs. get X% off). Never test message tone and send time simultaneously — you won't know which drove the result.

Deliverability Optimization

Carrier filtering is the invisible enemy. Messages get filtered for: spam-like URL patterns, excessive caps/punctuation, known spam phrases ("FREE!!!," "WINNER"), mismatched sender/content. To maintain deliverability:

  • Use branded short domains for links (sms.yourbrand.com) — never bitly or tinyurl
  • Keep character sets simple — avoid Unicode emojis in early sends until sender reputation is established
  • Warm up new 10DLC numbers: start at 200 msg/day, increase 20% per week
  • Monitor carrier complaint rates via your SMS provider dashboard — Twilio, Bandwidth, and Vonage all surface this data

Reporting Cadence

Weekly: opt-out rate, complaint rate, reply rate per active sequence. Monthly: attributed revenue by channel, sequence completion rates, list growth vs. churn. Quarterly: full program audit — retire sequences with <3% reply rate, refresh copy on sequences 90+ days old, audit compliance documentation.
